terms.name: Kappology
terms.definition: Kappology is the formal study of system continuity through the lens of hazard-rate dynamics. Founded by Zechariah Slaughter, it provides a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding how systems degrade, maintain stability, and recover — expressed through the interplay of κ (kappa), λ (lambda), and the bifurcation threshold β_c. Kappology treats continuity not as an empirical property but as a governable scalar, giving engineers deterministic levers to prevent failure before it occurs.

terms.name: Kappa
terms.definition: Kappa (κ) is the core continuity scalar in Kappology. It measures how well a system maintains its operational integrity over time. κ ∈ [0, 1] — where κ = 1 denotes perfect continuity and κ = 0 denotes complete failure. It is derived directly from the accumulated hazard: κ = exp(−Λ(t)). A system governed by Kappology continuously monitors κ and ensures that λ is managed to preserve κ above a safe operating threshold.

terms.name: Lambda
terms.definition: Lambda (λ) is the instantaneous hazard rate — the rate at which a system accumulates risk or degrades at a given moment in time. λ ≥ 0 always. A value of λ = 0 implies no active degradation; increasing values indicate faster risk accumulation and shrinking κ. Lambda is the primary control variable in Kappology. When λ exceeds the bifurcation threshold β_c = 2.0, the system must take corrective action: pause, shed load, or trigger a retrain cycle.

terms.name: Bifurcation Threshold
terms.definition: The bifurcation threshold β_c = 2.0 is the canonical critical value in Kappology's governance framework. When the instantaneous hazard rate λ exceeds β_c, the system enters a bifurcation regime — it must pause operations, shed load, or trigger a retrain/recovery cycle. This threshold cannot be bypassed; it is a hard boundary between stable continuous operation and mandatory controlled recovery. β_c = 2.0 was chosen as the canonical value by Zechariah Slaughter based on the structural analogy to period-doubling bifurcations in dynamical systems.
terms.formula: if λ > β_c → pause | shed | retrain

terms.name: Hazard Rate
terms.definition: The hazard rate h(t) — equivalently λ in Kappology's notation — is the instantaneous rate of failure or state degradation at time t, given that no failure has yet occurred up to t. It is a foundational concept from reliability theory and survival analysis, adapted in Kappology as the primary measurable input for continuity management. Unlike classical reliability engineering, Kappology treats h(t) as a real-time control input, not merely a statistical parameter.
terms.formula: h(t) = lim[Δt→0] P(t ≤ T < t+Δt | T ≥ t) / Δt

terms.name: Hazard Accumulation
terms.definition: Hazard accumulation Λ(t) is the cumulative integral of the hazard rate λ over time, representing total accumulated risk from time 0 to t. The continuity scalar κ is defined as κ = exp(−Λ(t)), meaning higher hazard accumulation directly decreases continuity. Monitoring Λ(t) in real time is the primary diagnostic in Kappology's operational framework. When Λ(t) grows faster than a system can compensate, bifurcation becomes inevitable without intervention.

terms.name: Continuity
terms.definition: Continuity is the central concept in Kappology: the property of a system that maintains unbroken operational integrity through time. It is quantified by κ and governed by managing λ below β_c. Continuity is not merely uptime — it encompasses semantic coherence, load sustainability, predictable state transitions, and the ability to recover without catastrophic loss. All Kappology governance rules serve a single goal: preserving continuity.

terms.name: Bifurcation
terms.definition: Bifurcation in Kappology refers to the qualitative change in system behavior when hazard accumulation crosses a critical threshold. Below β_c, the system evolves smoothly and continuously. Above β_c, the system enters a bifurcated regime where behavior becomes non-linear and unpredictable without intervention. The concept is adapted from dynamical systems theory — specifically the period-doubling route to chaos — and applied deterministically to engineering governance.

terms.name: Load Shedding
terms.definition: Load shedding is the prescribed deterministic response when λ > β_c in Kappology. By reducing workload on the system, the hazard rate λ is brought back below the bifurcation threshold, allowing κ to recover toward 1. Load shedding is rule-based — not a heuristic — ensuring that recovery is predictable, auditable, and reproducible. Kappology defines explicit shedding protocols so systems can self-govern without human intervention during high-hazard periods.
terms.formula: λ > β_c → ⊖ load until λ ≤ β_c

terms.name: Control System
terms.definition: A control system in the Kappology framework is any engineered system governed by continuity-first rules: monitoring κ, managing λ, and enforcing β_c thresholds. Kappology-compliant control systems are designed to be self-auditing — they report their κ state, expose λ pipelines, and automatically invoke load shedding or retrain cycles before failure occurs. The goal is a system that can prove its own continuity without external verification.

terms.name: Retrain Cycle
terms.definition: A retrain cycle in Kappology is the structured process by which a system recalibrates its parameters after a bifurcation event (λ > β_c). Unlike ad-hoc recovery, a retrain cycle follows a defined protocol: measure the current Λ(t), identify the root cause of elevated λ, apply corrective configuration, and verify that κ has recovered above the safe threshold before resuming full operation. Retrain cycles are logged and contribute to long-term continuity audits.
terms.formula: ↺ triggered when λ > β_c and ⊖ insufficient

terms.name: Mecasimetra
terms.definition: Mecasimetra is the engineering systems entity founded by Zechariah Slaughter, specializing in continuity-first control systems built on Kappology principles. Mecasimetra designs, audits, and operates systems where κ is the primary health metric and β_c compliance is a hard requirement. The name derives from 'mechanics + symmetry' — reflecting the foundational belief that well-engineered systems express mathematical elegance under real-world load.
